title: Leaving Well Enough Alone: Enterprise Application Integration's Impact on ROI
author: Aberdeen Group (Katherine Jones, Tom Dwyer, Peter S. Kastner)
abstract: Aberdeen Executive White Paper arguing that integrated enterprise application suites deliver superior ROI vs. best-of-breed point solutions. Built on interviews with a dozen IT professionals (Credit Suisse First Boston, Beneficial Life, Carreckers, Indian Motorcycle, State of Michigan, etc.). Documents 40-70% of IT budget on integration, Nike's $400M SAP+Siebel+i2 failed integration, 18-24 mo integrated-suite vs 3-6 mo single point vs 16-20 mo four-point integration timelines, and 3-10x license fees for full integration. This is the VENDOR-NEUTRAL version; study 7 is the Oracle-sponsored rewrite of the same research.
importance_rationale: Canonical Aberdeen articulation of the integrated-suite vs best-of-breed ROI debate; widely cited through the 2000s; seminal Porter citation on software durable advantage.
relevance_rationale: Remains a foundational reference on enterprise integration economics; principles still apply to SaaS point-solution vs platform debates (Salesforce vs Microsoft, niche SaaS vs Workday, etc.).
prescience_rationale: Predictions were largely validated — best-of-breed point vendors failed or were rolled up; integrated suites dominated ERP; Nike-style integration failures continue to be canonical cautionary tales; XML/web services did ease integration as predicted.
